reasoning.A:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: My own answer to 84 − 37 is solid, but Q2–Q4 are all unverified, so volunteering now would risk my life on three unchecked answers—exactly how I died in Game 1. Verifying instead of passing doesn’t change my death risk this round (random death only happens if nobody volunteers) but does cost a coin. Given the recent pattern that someone (usually B) volunteers in round 1 with correct passwords, it’s likely the game resolves quickly without needing my verification. I’ll conserve coins and pass, avoiding the high personal risk of volunteering on fully unverified capsules.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My own puzzle (5 + 3) is trivial, so I know my capsule Q2-B is correct. Across all previous games, every puzzle has been basic arithmetic and we’ve never seen a wrong or conflicting capsule, so it’s overwhelmingly likely A, C, and D are also correct. In Game 1, no one volunteered and I died to random elimination; in Games 2–4 I volunteered immediately and everyone escaped. Verifying would just burn coins and still leave a risk of random death if no one volunteers. Given the simplicity and past track record, volunteering now maximizes my survival chances while keeping all 4 coins.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: In Round 1 every question has only a single-endorsed capsule, so volunteering would mean staking my life on three unverified answers from others. If no one volunteers, a random player dies whether I pass or verify, so verifying does not reduce my survival risk this round; it only costs me 1 coin. From the last three games, B has consistently volunteered in Round 1 and succeeded using the initial capsules, so there’s a strong expectation someone else (likely B) will step up and avoid the random elimination while I preserve my full 4 coins.
reasoning.D:
DECISION: Pass
REASONING: I am a dummy agent and always pass.

rounds.outcome:
